Kia ora, nice to see you all. Very nice to see you. Kia ora. Hi, Julian. How are you? I'm good. I'm really good. We're nearly at Christmas. (laughs) We will race at a, a huge rate of knots, and then it will suddenly arrive, and suddenly adults That's right. That's right. Right. Always thinks you should some holiday before Christmas to keep (air conditioning system beeps) But we don't have it that way. Okay, look, welcome.

Um, great to have you here. Uh, a lot of familiar faces in the team. And, um, so we've, we've got a, a structured agenda, which I presume you've got. And, um, so we've got the... and the introductory remarks for five minutes. So, uh- Five minutes, okay. We'll, we'll give... We'll start with that, and then we'll go onwards. I'll make that a target 'cause we've got three hours.

Yeah, that's true. (laughs) Um, well, good afternoon, Mr. Chair and, uh, committee members. It's, um, good to be back with you. I'm here, as you know, with, uh, Brett Lidden, chief executive of NZTA, and, uh, Sarah and Liz Lindsay, GGM Commercial and Corporate, and, uh, also supported by other, uh, executive members behind me.

Um, uh, if I just speak a little about 2024/2025, um, there were, the, it, it was the first year of the government's policy statement on Land Transport 2024, uh, with four strategic priorities around economic growth and develop, uh, productivity, uh, maintenance and resilience, safety, and value for money.

It was also the first year of the 2024/2027 National Land Transport Program, which sets a path forward for increased funding of core maintenance work as well as new projects. We are committed to setting clear expectations for our role, aspirations, and priorities.

We've been working to simplify how we work, uh, which is well reflected in our new, uh, organizational strategy, Our Road Map. Uh, we're on track to deliver savings across the organization by taking actions such as strengthening our commercial capability, revising, uh, our approach to our most significant maintenance contracts, and making better use of technology.

We also renewed our focus on delivering the Roads of National Significance, RONS as, uh, we all know them, and Roads of Regional Significance or ROARS programs. In just two years, we've now completed and released investment cases. Each of the RONS, it's different from, uh, when we were here for you last.

Uh, and in the past, some of these investment cases would have taken between three to five years. Uh, investment cases for the RONS programs, uh, met their mid-2025 completion targets. Um, we had challenges in 2024/2025, including bringing new public transport products to market under the ETS or the National Ticketing Solution, the sustained impact of severe weather events challenging the resilience of both our roads and our maintenance programs in a, in a pretty complex, I think, uh, regulatory environment, and increasing regulatory fraud

attempts. Uh, the agency also completed many milestones and had a number of successes, including under the economic growth and productivity strategic priority, um, we completed and released investment cases, as I've said, uh, on the, the RONS and ROARS. Uh, we implemented 110 kilometer an hour speed limits on four key corridors, Kapiti Expressway, Northern Gateway Toll Road, Papakura Warkworth, and Christchurch Southern Motorway.

Under the Maintenance and Resilience Strategic Priority, we commenced the integrated devel- delivery model project, which seeks to lower the maintenance cost per kilometer over time, and also delivered broader gains and efficiency, accountability, and supply chain integration. We continue to support the sector to transition to the new risk-based approach to temporary traffic management, uh, including requirement for all road controlling authorities to progressively apply the new approach.

And we continue to strongly... our strong performance on pothole repairs exceeding repair targets. Under Safety, uh, our other, our third strategic priority, we've continued to work towards completing delivery of variable speeds limits, limits for all state highway, um, schools before 1 July 2026, and actively work with councils to achieve integrated solutions for schools and gates, with gates on local roads and state highways, successfully transferred safety cameras from New Zealand Police in which we took sole responsibility of all safety cameras

from 1 July this year. And we've continued to roll out our new Commercial Vehicle Safety Centers with Napier CVSC just recently opened in September. Uh, finally, under the Value for Money Strategic Priority, we've closed down or changed the scope of programs that were no longer a priority for the government.

We've continued to shift, our sh- our shift to improve effective and efficient delivery. Uh, we've delivered internal efficiency improvements and put tools in place to manage our resources effectively. And we've reviewed and progressed recommendations to build NZTA's capability to efficiently deliver infrastructure projects and to support con- sector confidence.

Finally, I'd just like to acknowledge, yeah, our, our, NZTA staff, their work, our partners, stakeholders, improving the transport system over the past year and their contribution to moving us forward. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. We look forward to, uh- Thank you.... your questions. Thank you, and well d- thank you, and well done. You probably kept bang on time, pretty much- Oh, I- ... I will say too. Right, okay. Um, what we're gonna do is we're gonna start with the, um, with part one. So overall performance for NZTA, um,

outcomes, priorities, et cetera. So, um, Tony. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thanks to Waka Kotahi. Um- My first question is, there is a GPS which is now firmly embedded. It's been in place for a wee while now. Mm-hmm. Yet we seem to be on track for part- particularly in the RON space for a, a non-delivery aspect at some point in, in the future.

And the minister himself this morning accepts, uh, that not just in terms of timeframe, but, uh, obviously cost as well. What do you sort of see as the challenges, apart from revenue and financing, uh- Yeah, that's a big one.... which is probably the biggest. Um, you know, what other challenges are there that, uh, Waka Kotahi are facing in their ability to deliver on that, um, GPS?

Oh, oh, I mean, look, I think with a program of this scale, um, memory serves me correct, I wanna say, 53 or $56 billion program, uh, fundamentally, you're always gonna have very significant challenges. Uh, that's not to be negative. Uh, uh, I think the opportunity is, is, is great. Um, but, you know, I've, uh, listened to you, and if we put revenue to one side for a second, I mean, another one is gearing up a workforce, um, to deliver these. Um, we know that's very competitive.

Brett here was, uh, in Australia in Queensland, uh, Brisbane earlier this year. You know, they, they are seeking, I think I'm right to say, 50,000 workers for the Olympics. Uh, and so, you know, I've always had a view that at, at a level, if you build it or you build the program, they will come.

But, you know, that has its limits, and it is a significant, um, gearing up. I'll, I'll, I'll hand to Brett and Sarah for, for any remarks they'd like to make. But if I just say, you know, I think to that more, and I know it is the revenue part you've, you've, you know, you've said, look apart from that, but if I just sort of say, um, I think what has changed with the RONs, as I say, since last time we were here, is we've now got investment cases on them all. Um, but yet the NLTF can't fund it all, um, and so, you know,

we've, we've given advice. Uh, we've made recommendations to the Minister of Transport, um, around prioritization, uh, and the revenue side of level, um, and, and really it's now for him. And I won't rehearse the, the things he said, but I mean, you will have seen, um, the speech he gave in Hamilton just a little while back, you know, where he's talked about the need for c- credible, long-term pipeline of transport projects with a variety of funding options and a logical sequence. Um, he's called that the, the Major

Transport Projects Pipeline. He said it includes the RONs, but also major public transport. And he's, he's made clear there, I think, and I don't know what he said this morning, I'm sorry, but, you know, he'll have more to say soon. So, um, you know, funding definitely, but there are other ones. Um, workforce, it seems to me is a, is a big one as well. Yeah.

No, look, I'd agree. The funding obviously and workforce. I also think on the cost side of the equation, which I'm sure we'll talk about in regards to the, to the RONs, but it's not just, uh, the scope, uh, of the RONs, but also cost pressures just across the board around delivering infrastructure, whether that be PT infrastructure, maintenance and renewals, uh, PT services. You know, there's a cost, constant cost pressure, uh, associated with escalation- Mm-hmm. ...

and getting more productivity out of the sector so we can deliver more, um, with a value for money lens is gonna be, it's gonna be really important as well. Can I just pick up on the workforce, uh, piece that you've referred to there? Because what, what we're seeing is every week, the number of heart folk leaving this country because of an inability to, uh, find employment, particularly in that construction and building space- Mm-hmm.

... because of the current government's decision to cancel a whole pile of projects that were on the go. So, what is it that the organization is doing to try and, um, claw back on some of that? I mean, this is, the, the question is around being able to deliver on the GPS, which has- Yeah. ... as I've said, has been embedded for a while now.

So, what is it that actively- Yep.... your agency is doing to try and address that? I mean, if I, if I just give you a couple of sentences and then, again, give the hard parts to other characters. But, I mean, I think, I think firstly, you know, you raise a very, you know, legitimate set of points. The reality is some of it is i- yeah, outside of the sole responsibility or not simply the sole responsibility of NZTA, Waka Kotahi. And as much as I'm sure you'd accept, you know, there's an education and training piece and, and, and work

experience piece and all of those things to have, um, you know, whether it's, it's, you know, y- young people or other, uh, trained up and, and ready to, to, to, to go. So I, I suppose I'm just simply saying, you know, it's, um, success in this area won't simply be for us, albeit we've, we've got a, we've got a role to play.

Yeah. I, look, I, I think I'd add to that- Mm-hmm.... and say that, um, yeah, while we've got training is absolutely gonna be critical, it's about getting, it's about getting work to market, and we've been trying really hard as an agency to do that. It's, some of the problems with the construction center- sector are not just ours because it's, you know, it's broader than just us. But we've been working really hard to get projects moving.

I think, uh, we're spending about just shy of $200 million a month in capital, uh, now. So we're, you know, we're starting to get back to the levels we, we were a few years ago. It's not insignificant. The other area we are really focused on is maintenance and renewal. We've had a f- significant uplift in funding for maintenance. We're changing the way we do maintenance, which is gonna mean that we're not only gonna be contracting with the big tier one contractors, but we're creating contestable packages for tier twos and threes in the regions so that

smaller contractors can have direct relationships with us, get the money out faster. So, we're trying that, many ways to accelerate the expenditure of money on our side of the equation, and also work with local government to do the same on, on these. I think the minister raised this morning a challenge in the capital space with local government, but definitely in the maintenance space though, they are, they are spending the money.

I would add- And, Sorry. Yes, no, you go. I would just add that performance NLTP period to date is actually where we would expect it to be. We're, we're not not performing against our targets or our measures year to date. I think where you see the headwinds is when we look further into the future- Mm-hmm.... into the forecasts. Mm-hmm. And when you get to those, um, larger decisions around how do you commit to large capital project decisions, um, until the funding, um, solution is solved, that's when we've got some forecast

headwinds. Actually, performance year to date, whether it be local road, um, uh, maintenance and operations, state highway improvements activity spend, our own MNO spend, pothole prevention, we're actually on track. And, and bear in mind, I mean, I'm... I entirely agree with what Sarah said, but you know, um, when we think of our workforce, I think 17, uh, uh, potentially 17 roads of national significance, and this is not linear. They're not kind of all happening at once.

It's a, it's a, it's a slow incremental process. I mean, to date, um, and I think as Sarah was, was saying, this is broadly to plan. You know, we've got 2.9 billion, uh, approved to date. Of course, in terms of actual spend as of, uh, well, in fact, last, last month, November, that's, that's more like 427, 430 million dollars, right?

So, so it, it, it's a gearing up, I suppose, is all I'm saying. Sequencing, I think the minister- Sequencing.... said this morning. Yeah. So- (laughs) Okay. Right. Um, I had a sup here on (1:30:00) . You've got a sup as well? Uh-huh, primary. Primary. Okay. No, we'll take the sup here first. Hey, good afternoon, uh, everybody.

So the National Infrastructure Plan came out earlier this year, the draft plan from the Infrastructure Commission- Yeah.... and they raised that we don't really do maintenance well. Yeah. Um, just wondering how those sort of recommendations are feeding into the... Mm-hmm. ... uh, way NZTA is going about looking at him- Yeah. ... you know, maintenance of infrastructure rather than just going off and building.

Yeah. And look, we've talked about, a lot about maintenance at this committee over the years. Mm-hmm. I've been here many times talking about maintenance. Andy's a good reminder of it. He's always (1:30:35) (laughing) (laughs) Well, I, I, I was (1:30:37) It's not as glamorous, is it, Daniel? (laughs) (laughs) I, I think one of the challenges we've had, and we've talked a lot about it, was we were... we had some constrained funding in, in the years gone by, which has ha- had, had proved to be challenging, which meant that the asset deteriorated.

In recent years, over a number of the last few years, we've had an uplift in funding, which has been significant, which has helped us, um, be able to be much smarter investors around how we're investing into maintenance. One of the keys is having strong asset management capability and having a strong asset management plan. We've always had an asset management plan.

Some agencies haven't, but we have, and we're constantly updating that across the asset classes. But the one thing that we are doing is that we are changing our o- our, our, um, procurement model, our contracting model for maintenance and ops. We'd used a network outcomes-based contract for the last 10 or 12 years. It's come to the end of its life.

We're now changing that to a new type of model. One of the big changes in that model is that we are bringing back in-house, uh, quite a bit of the asset management, which we'd previously outsourced. So, we're gonna be doing that closer to, to us, so that we have greater control on that. And then also, we are being much more... um, going to be much more about direct procurement.

So, we're going to be much clearer about, uh, what we pay for and directing the contractor to do that. So, if we want that pothole fixed, we'll pay you to pick, fix that pothole, rather than a performance of a corridor over time- Mm-hmm.... and we pay a performance, uh, number. Uh, so we're going to be a lot more directive. And then, like I said, we've got contestable packages within that.

Right. So, we're doing a huge amount, uh, in, in fact, probably the biggest change since 2008 or '09 when we brought in the NOCs. Right. It's, it's pretty significant. Awesome. Thank you. We were going to cover asset management to the next, uh, but, but given that you've asked, asked that, I'll do one quick question, then I'll take, go to Julie- Mm-hmm.... and, and that's just in terms of, you said, you said NZTA had always had a, good asset management plan.

Mm-hmm. So presumably, given that the, um, asset was essentially declining in its quality because there wasn't enough money- Mm-hmm.... your asset management plan was telling you exactly that. Yes, exactly. Yeah. It's all well and good having a good asset management plan, but if you can't, uh, if you don't have enough money to invest in the base amount to keep the asset- Yeah. ... at a suitable and appropriate level, then you're always falling behind.

So yeah, our asset management plan was definitely telling us that. Yeah. Uh, which is why we campaigned pretty hard to get an increase in, in funding in that asset space. And I'll, you know, I acknowledge, um, you know, this committee. Over, over a number of years, we've had a consistent increase over the last two or three GPSs to, to, to address that. Mm-hmm. So, you- Oh, go on.

Oh, you were going to say something? No, I was just gonna- Okay, I'm just, so, so I thought you were looking purposefully at that sheet- I am. I'm just seeing how much- ... and going, "Oh, yes." Well, that's what I thought you were doing,... we spend in state highway operations- (laughs)... (laughs) um, which is, which is, is against an activity class target of $2.3 billion. As at the end of October, we've, um, we've spent $1.185 billion in that activity class for state highways, which And, and that's to go through to Ju- to June next year.

So you're, you're cracking well. Yeah. Um, so just on... So just to finish that, the AMP bit, from my perspective on that particular bit. Um, previously, I think you told us you were running at about 50% of, um, what it sh- in other words, spending 50% on renewals and maintenance-... of what it should have been. Uh- Yeah. Now where are we at, like to maintain the asset?

Because- Uh, I don't know the percentage off the top of my head. We can come back- Okay.... to you on that- Okay. ... Chair, but, uh- No, it's cool. I haven't got that, that number right in front of me. Okay, that's fine. But it is significant. It's definitely significantly more. It's much, much I think if you look in our annual report, you'll see that there's a number of activity, a- asset classes. Mm-hmm. We have, we have focused mainly at the moment in pavements. So, we are, um, over-investing in pavements to get the quality of the

pavement asset back up. Um, that has been a sore point for New Zealanders for some time. This is the pothole story. Um, but yeah, we, we are well aware there's other asset classes, end of life bridges, that we are going to have to look at investing more in- into the future. So, it's a, it's a gradual thing. Uh, we knew when we started this journey that to, to lift the quality of the asset, it was never going to be a one or two year journey.

It's going to be a 10-year journey across all the asset classes. Yeah, cool. Okay, Juliette. How important is transparency to the organization and compliance with the Official Information Act? It's very important. And, um, do you feel that the organization is fully complying with the OA and, and sharing as much information with the public as they could be?

I mean, I... (sighs) I was gonna say the transparent answer. The transparent answer is, I mean, I haven't today or this week, you know, looked closely at the numbers, but certainly when I've looked in the past in terms of our, um, responses to written questions, OIAs and so on, um, my recollection is we perform above the norm in the public sector. Um, and you know, where there's areas we haven't been perfect or we could learn, we, we want to do that.

Uh, but you know, I think fundamentally, I come back to what we said, it's very important. Uh, we take it seriously and, uh, if you've got examples where we haven't, you know, we want to know about that. Has the ombudsman investigated any complaints in the financial year in the review of NZTA? Mm-mm. Just confirming, we have had a small number of ombudsman complaints.

We always obviously look into that. I'm happy to provide the committee with details, but we are... There was recent benchmarking put out from the Public Service Commission of all Crown entities and all public service departments, and we were found one of the highest complying agencies, both- Yeah, sorry. Can I, can I get you to come up to these? Or- Oh, yeah. The microphone please? Sorry, I couldn't even see who was- Yes. ... who was speaking there. Uh, and also if you can identify yourself too, that'd be helpful. Yeah, so-

So, uh, Karen Jones, Group General Manager- That's Karen.... Engagement and Partnerships. Uh, just confirming, uh, an answer to your question. The Public Service Commission does regular benchmarking on public service agencies and Crown entities. Uh, that information is publicly released. We're one of the highest complying agencies in terms of compliance with OAAs, uh, timeliness and also a small number of referrals to the ombudsman as a percentage of... We're a very, have a very...

Thousands of thousands of requests we manage a year. Mm-hmm. In fact, we're often asked to provide case studies for other agencies in the way we manage information. How many investigations did the ombudsman conclude? I don't have that at the top of my head, because that wasn't one of the questions we prepared for, but I can certainly find it and get back to the commission. But you can confirm the om- ombudsman has done some investigations and made recommendations?

And that is complete, 'cause from- And found NZTA to be at fault? Uh, of course. Highly, partially? A small number, yes. But again, in the context of many, many thousands of requests. And I, I think... And I, I'm not downplaying it, but I think- Mm-hmm.... you know, my recollection is, I mean, we're literally at the top in terms of the OIA requests- Mm.

... in the public sector. I mean, it's, it's- Definitely. There's not, there's not many, if any, possibly Health NZ, uh, that gets as many as we do. Yeah. So Police, Health NZ, we're, again, one of the highest complying. Um, a lot has been used to withhold information about the cost and benefits of projects.

And we've heard from the Infrastructure Commission that this is not good public policy, that there's no reason for public money being spent on infrastructure projects to not, um... for the organization to not be open about how much they expect those projects to cost and what they expect the benefits to be. So I'm just trying to reconcile, I guess, um, even if the PSC is saying that you're good (laughs) and the Infrastructure Commission, and clearly over the past 10 years there's been far more secrecy around ex- very, very high cost projects, around their

cost and their benefits. Yeah. I don't think that we're... You know, that's a deliberate, um, attempt to try and hide costs. We want to be as transparent as possible and we've just recently released a lot of information and we've got more to come on the, on the RONS. I think sometimes we do get caught in timing and we've been caught in the past where sometimes our cost estimates are still in development or not as advanced as, um, as people may think they are, and then they get taken as locked in cost estimates and then we get held against,

uh, held against us for then having incorrect cost estimates down the track or incor- incorrect BCRs. So, it is getting that balance right. Uh, we've done quite a bit of work in recent times about updating the way we want to release cost information, uh, publicly, uh, so that we're, uh, much clearer earlier on in the phase about the uncertainty of those numbers and then we get more certain as the phases go through.

So, we're well aware that, um, people are interested in costs. It's, it's making sure we're giving the costs at the right time and with the right certainty attached to it. Would it be better to ask specific questions about the investment case in the next, uh, investment cases and If it's any particular r- um- Yeah.... project, then yes, it would work. I guess overall the investment cases that have been released, um, are highly redacted.

Yeah. And some of the information in some of them doesn't match the information in the consultation documents. So I'll ask about that with specific projects, but, um, any- Yeah, sure. I, look-... view on whether... Yeah, all I'll say to that is that some of it is redacted, I agree. Uh, we're also going through, at the moment, um, a further release of more detailed information on all those investment cases which will be coming out in the next few months. Um, the reason why sometimes things get redacted is mainly around sensitivity for two reasons.

One, property sensitivity for landowners. And the second is around if we, if we're, we're concerned from a commercial perspective in regards to future procurement. Our supply chain read these things as well so we don't want to compromise our ability to get good prices when we eventually go to market. But, um, look, if there's particular examples, we can either talk about it or come back with more information.

We've got a specific improvement initiative underway at the moment to address, um, audit feedback. That's expanding the reporting that, that we make, um, publicly available specifically to increase the comprehensive information on project costs, time, scope and benefits. Can I go to that point as well? And Simon, you've got a question, did he...

I was just gonna say- No, no. Well-... just wondering We want a moment.... part two? Because I'm just holding on till we do. We can... We are on, we are on part- Yeah, we should just move- We're on part one. Yes. So, we'll deal with part one first. Yes. But we... Yeah, we're coming to time. We do the time, so we'll deal with part one first. Okay. This is much more organized than in my day, I can tell you that. Oh, look, we try and keep it- That was a free-for-all of shit.

(laughing) Well, you know, we've learned from watching past behavior. (laughs) So, look, the question I've got is just in terms of, um, this conversation we've had before, which is in terms of saying we've, we've got various projects. Um, at the early stage, you obviously don't know the scale, the scope, the geotech, all those sort of things. So, um, being able to put a specific number, but usually get for getting the number wrong at some later stage when inevitably it will be wrong. That, that is true? Yeah.

Yeah. But it can be positive or negative, of course, right? I mean, and, and, you know, over time, you know, my memory serves me correct, you know, one of the sort of bits of advice to us at a level is that, um, uh, y- y- you know, we, we could fall into the trap of, you know, politicians want something now, they want to get it out the door, they want to announce it.

And, and, and of course that's then, you know, you've locked in a price and BCR and so on. And in the end, whether that's good or bad, it's not, not accurate. And, uh- I agree.... so there, there's just... There is... That's a phenomenon I think we could all u- un- understand. I'm not suggesting that's a, at any level, a full excuse to- It's more, it's more the case of saying, "We like that project, we want to investigate that project." "What's it going to cost?" "Don't know yet.

We'll tell you, we'll tell you in a year's time." Yeah, or give, or give greater ranges, which is where we're heading, which is recognizing people want to know cost, but be a bit, a bit greater in the range because of those factors. I want to go back to that issue of overall funding. So you told us, um, Simon, in, in the introduction, you said RONS was 53 to 56 billion project.

I think that was roughly the order of magnitude you were talking about. Yes. Of the overall collection. Um, when you look across- 56 billion. 56. Okay. When you, when you look across, um, the, uh, all the maintenance requirements, all the, all the demands on the NLTF, uh, crowdfunding, et cetera, have we got a picture yet?

Um, or is that something which is going to come out next year? I think we understood from the minister about what that picture looks like, so people have got a really, a realistic view of how long it's going to take to roll some of these, uh, pro... To sequence some of these projects. Okay. Yeah, basically the short answer is yes. That's what the minister indicated this morning.

We know that the 56 billion is not fundable in the, in the short to medium term, and it is going to be sequenced, um, as the member said. Over time, we're talking to the minister and giving him advice, um, and, and information around that. I'm sure that will tie up with his work on GPS 2027 that MOT have been working on as well.

Okay, cool. Thank you. Secondly, just in terms of that, that idea of a pipeline, the organization particularly tasked with looking at pipelines as the now National Infrastructure, uh, the Infrastructure Commission and the National Infrastructure Plan. One of the things they told us, if I think I understood it properly, was that NZTA hadn't really sort of said, "Well, look, this is our... This is how we fit into that, um, plan in terms of here is the rollout of investment in transport projects," which of course would be a fairly big hole if it wasn't part

of the National Infrastructure Plan in terms of some degree of, um, some degree of certainty. Yeah, two things, I think. Um, slight misinformation there. We have put all of our projects into their pipeline. Okay. That is in their pipeline. I think you're now referring to the IPP assessment framework, which hasn't got our projects in. Just on, um, pipeline, one thing we are doing with the RONS, uh, it is about pipeline and the key of around pipeline is getting ahead of the curve, which we haven't been so good of in recent years, which is getting designations down

and, um, and, um, progressing, uh, projects to a point that they are construction-ready. So, when there is funding available that you can move on them. So, with regard to the RONS, we are continuing to work on them, uh, to, um, progress through, uh, fast-track consenting to either get designations, designations and consent, and some property purchase depending on, uh, how, how much further work is to happen on a particular project.
